text: Finally you close your laptop, and I bring you a glass of the overly sweet wine you love but I hate, and the floor is gritty with the salt you poured at the thresholds. The air tastes of the ocean, and I bring my tongue to my lips again and again, thinking of how later, in the dark, I’ll kneel between your legs in worship. I stroke your hair and watch as you swallow. I can feel the ghost upstairs expand like a gust of wind, a groan caught in its non-existent throat. You don’t hear it this time, but your arms break out in goose bumps. “They updated the county numbers today. Ninety more positive. Ten dead,” you say. “You have to stop reading the news. It’s morbid.” You shrug. Sip your wine. “So is the entire fucking world right now.” Somewhere in the house, a door opens, and there is the sound of something small moving carefully. You freeze, your glass lifted halfway to your lips, your eyes darting to the scattered line of salt. “I didn’t put any upstairs yet.” “We can sleep down here tonight if you want. The couch pulls out.” “Okay. Yeah.” You nod. From upstairs comes a muffled scream, the sound of someone with a hand clamped over her mouth, and then a thud. I’ve read that a poltergeist is a kind of imprint of what’s come before, but I carry too many identical memories to identify which transgression this sound belongs to. “There are spare blankets in the downstairs closet,” I say and stand. You stay where you are, your shoulders rigid as you listen for another reminder that I’ve brought you into a horror story. Somehow, you are even more beautiful when you are frightened, and I want to pull you onto the floor and tug off your panties, but you wouldn’t allow it. Not now. Not with a ghost over our heads, and the secret of what it is burning inside me. There was never any question of your being out. You grew up surrounded by love, parents who looked at you and saw their own hearts walking through the world. There were girlfriends before me. Other people who loved you. It was inevitable. I’m harder to love than you, and I think you saw me as something to be conquered. You could not have known how I once dreamed of living the truth you’d always known, the lost girl in me gnashing her teeth in hunger. The blankets in the closet smell of mothballs, and you wrinkle your nose as I shake them out and smooth the edges over the couch’s small mattress. We settle in, the television casting blue shadows over the room. You asked me to leave it on, the muted susurrations a semblance of safety. Eventually, you slip into a restless sleep, so I turn the television off and wait. I don’t have to wait long. The energy in the room shifts, the air gone thick in the way I recognize, and I close my eyes. Tell myself that whatever I see will not be a horror. “It’s only me,” I whisper and open my eyes into the dark. It’s standing in front of me, its mouth open and hanging loose, the jaw unhinged from the socket. It appears as a girl, as me at twelve, but it isn’t. It smells of ozone. “He broke our jaw. I remember,” I say. It tilts its head and reaches for me, tangles its fingers in my hair, which I’d finally cut short. Even after I’d left this house, my father, behind, I’d think of cutting it and feel hellfire licking at my feet for daring to consider such an abomination. A woman’s hair was her modesty. Her glory. It drops its hand, and its mouth works uselessly, a thin whine fading into a hiss. “You can leave now. He’s dead,” I say, but it looks beyond me to you. You turn over and sigh, and the sound breaks me open in the way it always does. I blink, and it is beside you, its face lowered to your lips, its mouth impossibly wide as it breathes in. Tasting you. Pulling you into itself and away from me. I scream your name, scramble toward you, and you come awake with a gasp, your hands clutching my arm as my ghost disappears back into the dark. “It’s okay. Just a bad dream,” I say as you burrow into my chest, saying again and again that you want out of this fucking house. I don’t tell you what I heard. What the ghost said just as you were coming awake. Mine . # You’ve stopped eating. You pretend to be full after a few bites and spend the rest of our paltry meals asking me question after question as if by filling your mouth with words, I won’t notice how you aren’t filling it with food. The house has been quiet, and we’ve gone back to the bedroom. You sleep clinging to the edge, every part of your body arching away from me, and I wrap the blanket around me, but it’s a paltry substitution for the heat of your body. You log in to your Zoom meetings, but you are quiet, letting the conversations, the work, exist without you. One morning, I catch you dumping your once beloved coffee down the sink, and I hold my hand to your head. You swat it away and frown. “I’m not sick.” “Something’s off. We should get you tested.” “For what exactly? We haven’t left this fucking house in weeks.” “The grocery deliveries. Something could have come in.” You snort and turn away. “I’m not getting my brain swabbed over nothing.” “People are dying. It’s not nothing.” “I said I’m fine. Jesus Christ.” I recoil from the force in your voice, and for a moment, it isn’t you but my father, the force of his hand bearing down on me. My heart accelerates,
